title: Sarah Paine - Why Putin and Xi can't escape geography
summary: Military historian Sarah Paine presents a two-thousand-year geopolitical framework dividing world powers into continental empires pursuing security through territorial conquest (China, Russia) and maritime powers pursuing prosperity through trade (Britain, US). She argues this framework explains modern behavior including Putin's geopolitical objectives.
writeup: A Naval War College lecturer argues that continental and maritime powers organize global influence through fundamentally different logics, and that this distinction still governs great-power competition today. The talk traces American history from a land-hungry continental expander to an emergent maritime power, introduces the canonical geopoliticians (Mahan, MacKinder, Spykman), and shows how China and Russia fail Mahan's prerequisites for a sustainable maritime paradigm. The through-line is that geography is not background: it dictates whether a state can defend at sea, fund commerce, and project power across oceans.

key_points: Mahan identified five prerequisites for a maritime power: a natural moat against land invasion, a dense internal transportation grid for peacetime commerce, reliable sea egress for the navy in wartime, a dense coastal population to run commerce, and stable government institutions that fund a navy and enforce commercial law. China and Russia today lack the full list.

MacKinder's 1904 Heartland thesis holds that Russia's pivot area in Eurasia is insulated by mountains, deserts, frozen seas, and inaccessible rivers, making it impervious to sea power and granting defense in depth and self-sufficiency that no maritime power can easily attack.

Spykman, writing in 1943, argued that US access to the Old World depended entirely on seaborne communication across the Atlantic and Pacific, and that whoever controls the rimland of the Eurasian heartland determines what foreign policy the United States can actually execute. He concluded that alliances providing continental bases are indispensable.

The United States began as a classic continental power: it used checkbook diplomacy (Louisiana Purchase 1803, Alaska purchase, Gadsden Purchase) where sellers accepted, and massed armies where they did not (Mexican-American War for Texas), all in pursuit of reaching the Pacific coast. The shift toward a maritime paradigm came later, driven by Mahan's influence.

Continental powers (elephants) must maintain competent armies because they cannot defend themselves at sea; maritime powers (whales) can in principle defend primarily with navies. From this single distinction flow downstream differences in economics, political institutions, and alliance strategy.

China's coastal population faces a narrow, island-clustered sea that is blockaded in wartime, and Xi Jinping's privileging of the crony sector over the private sector undermines the commerce-driven economy Mahan required. Russia's only reliable open-ocean egress is in the Arctic, where population and commerce are negligible.

chapters.title: Sun Tzu and China as Original Land Power
